county_metadata.record_losses: Some early territorial records lost when county seat moved from Mohave City to Hardyville to Mineral Park to Kingman
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of four original Arizona Territory counties (1864). County seat moved multiple times: Mohave City (1864), Hardyville (1867), Cerbat (1871), Mineral Park (1873), Kingman (1887). Contains Fort Mohave Indian Reservation, Hualapai Reservation, and Havasupai Reservation (partially).
county_metadata.research_tips: Mohave is one of Arizona's four original territorial counties (1864), but early records are sparse due to multiple county seat relocations. Kingman became permanent county seat in 1887 when the railroad arrived. County Recorder in Kingman has deeds — earliest surviving records date to approximately 1871. Mining was central: Cerbat Range, Oatman gold district, and chloride mining camps generated claim records. Route 66 history is significant for 20th-century research. The Mohave Museum of History and Arts in Kingman holds local collections. Fort Mohave Indian Reservation (Mohave tribe) and Hualapai Reservation records are held by respective tribal offices. Steamboat landing records along the Colorado River (Hardyville) are at ASLAPR. The Mohave County Miner newspaper (Mineral Park/Kingman, from 1882) is partially digitized.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index available online; some images through county recorder

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from approximately 1871. Some gaps in earliest territorial period due to county seat moves.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some early records lost in county seat relocations; generally intact from 1871

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from early 1870s. Some gaps in earliest territorial records.
Wills, estates, guardianships. Mining claim probate records document complex property transfers.
Published from Mineral Park then Kingman. Mining news, legal notices, community events, and territorial politics.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Mohave County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for homestead, desert land, and mining entries in Mohave County.
Federal census from 1870 (first Arizona territorial census). Sparse population in early enumerations. Indian reservation schedules separate.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1895 from Apache County. Contains portions of Navajo Nation and Hopi Reservation. Named for the Navajo people. Snowflake, Taylor, Show Low, and Pinetop-Lakeside are communities outside reservation lands.
county_metadata.research_tips: Navajo County has two distinct research contexts: the Mormon farming communities (Snowflake, Taylor, Holbrook) and the Navajo Nation/Hopi reservation lands. For Mormon families, LDS ward records from the Snowflake Stake (organized 1887) are essential — Snowflake and Taylor were founded by LDS settlers including the Flake and Smith families. County Recorder in Holbrook has deeds from 1895. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. The Navajo Nation records office in Window Rock (in Apache County but administers records for Navajo communities in Navajo County) is essential for tribal members. Hopi Tribe Cultural Preservation Office in Kykotsmovi handles Hopi records. Petrified Forest and Painted Desert homesteading records at BLM. Pre-1895 records are in Apache County, St. Johns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index available online through county recorder

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1895 to present. Off-reservation land transactions; ranching and farming properties.
Marriage licenses from 1895.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1895.
Snowflake Ward, Taylor Ward, Show Low Ward, and other congregations. One of the earliest LDS stakes in Arizona (organized 1887). Extensive membership and ordinance records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved in LDS Church archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: First census as Navajo County in 1900. Pre-1895 under Apache County. Navajo and Hopi reservation populations on separate schedules.
Local newspapers covering ranching, railroad (ATSF), and Mormon community events. Legal notices and land transactions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of four original Arizona Territory counties (1864). Cochise, Graham, and Santa Cruz counties carved from Pima. Tucson served as territorial capital 1867-1877. Contains Tohono O'odham Nation (second-largest reservation in US), Pascua Yaqui Reservation, and San Xavier District of Tohono O'odham Nation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pima County, centered on Tucson, has the deepest historical record in Arizona — Tucson was a Spanish presidio (1775) and Mexican town before American acquisition. San Xavier del Bac mission records (Franciscan, from 1692) at the Diocese of Tucson are among the oldest in Arizona. Spanish and Mexican-era records (baptisms, marriages, burials, land grants) are critical for pre-American families. County Recorder in Tucson has deeds from 1864. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. The Arizona Historical Society main branch in Tucson is the state's premier historical research center with extensive manuscript collections. University of Arizona Special Collections has territorial-era documents. Tohono O'odham Nation and Pascua Yaqui Tribe maintain separate records. Tucson city directories from 1881 at AHS. Pima County was the parent of Cochise, Graham, and Santa Cruz counties — researchers should check Pima County for earlier records of those areas.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online search portal with index and document images from 1864 to present

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1864 to present. Online portal provides grantor/grantee search and document images. Oldest continuous deed records in Arizona.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact; some of Arizona's earliest county records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1864 — among the earliest county marriage records in Arizona. Pre-1864 marriages recorded in church registers.
Wills, estates, and guardianships from 1864. Includes probate for daughter counties (Cochise, Graham, Santa Cruz) before their formation.

source_profiles.source_name: San Xavier del Bac Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Tucson / University of Arizona
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Franciscan mission records including baptisms, marriages, and burials of O'odham, Yaqui, and Spanish/Mexican families. Among the oldest church records in Arizona. Some records microfilmed by the Huntington Library.

source_profiles.source_name: Arizona Daily Star and Tucson Citizen
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tucson's major newspapers. Arizona Daily Star from 1877 and Tucson Citizen from 1870. Legal notices, obituaries, territorial politics.

source_profiles.source_name: Spanish and Mexican Land Grants — Pima County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mexican-era land grants in the Tucson area, confirmed or rejected by the Court of Private Land Claims (1891-1904). Baca Float, Tumacacori grant, San Ignacio de la Canoa, and others.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records at NARA; surveys at BLM

source_profiles.coverage_notes: 1860 census (as part of New Mexico Territory), 1864 territorial census, and federal census 1870-1950. Early censuses document Spanish/Mexican families predating American control.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 from Pima County. Named for the Pinal Mountains. Contains Gila River Indian Community (partially), Ak-Chin Indian Community, and portions of San Carlos Apache Reservation and Tohono O'odham Nation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pinal County is centered on Florence, one of Arizona's oldest Anglo settlements (1866). The Florence courthouse (1878, rebuilt 1891) is a historic landmark. County Recorder in Florence has deeds from 1875; some pre-county records exist from the Florence community. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. The Pinal County Historical Society and Museum in Florence is an excellent local resource. Silver King Mine near Superior generated significant mining records. The Arizona State Prison in Florence (opened 1909) has inmate records at ASLAPR. Casa Grande Ruins National Monument area has both archaeological and early homesteading records. Gila River Indian Community and Ak-Chin Indian Community maintain tribal records separately. The town of Superior and nearby mining communities have separate community histories tied to the Magma Copper Company.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and images available through county recorder; online access expanding

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1875. Mining claims for Silver King and other districts included.
Marriage licenses from 1875.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1875.

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Clerk of Superior Court, Florence
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial-era court records including criminal cases, civil disputes, and naturalization proceedings.
Florence newspapers covering territorial politics, mining, agriculture, and community events. Legal notices and land entries.
Federal census from 1880. Pre-1875 area enumerated under Pima County. Florence community appears in 1870 census under Pima.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1899 from Pima and Cochise counties. Border county — Nogales (AZ) directly adjoins Nogales (Sonora, Mexico). Contains Tumacacori National Historical Park (Spanish mission). Arizona's smallest county by area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Cruz County is a border county with deep Spanish/Mexican roots — Tumacacori mission (established 1691), Tubac presidio (1752, first European settlement in Arizona), and Nogales on the US-Mexico border. Spanish colonial church records from Tumacacori and Guevavi missions are at the University of Arizona and Diocese of Tucson. Mexican-era records (1821-1853, Gadsden Purchase) may be in Sonoran archives (Archivo General del Estado de Sonora, Hermosillo). County Recorder in Nogales has deeds from 1899; pre-1899 records in Pima and Cochise counties. US customs and immigration records at Nogales port of entry (NARA) are essential for cross-border families. Patagonia and Sonoita ranching records document early Anglo settlers. Santa Cruz Valley Heritage Alliance has local history collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Limited online access; in-person research at county recorder recommended

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1899. Includes ranching properties and cross-border land transactions.
Marriage licenses from 1899. Cross-border marriages common; Mexican church marriages may predate civil records.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1899.

source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Tucson / University of Arizona / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish Franciscan mission records — baptisms, marriages, burials of O'odham and Spanish/Mexican settlers. Guevavi (1691), Tumacacori, and Calabazas mission records are among Arizona's oldest.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragile originals; microfilm copies at various repositories

source_profiles.coverage_notes: US customs manifests, border crossing records, and immigration case files at the Nogales port of entry. Essential for Mexican-American families.

source_profiles.source_name: Spanish and Mexican Land Grants — Santa Cruz County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mexican-era land grants adjudicated by the Court of Private Land Claims (1891-1904). Baca Float No. 3, Tumacacori grant, San Jose de Sonoita, and others.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records at NARA; surveys at BLM

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of four original Arizona Territory counties (1864). Parent county of Apache (1879), Coconino (1891), and Maricopa (1871). Prescott was first territorial capital (1864-1867, again 1877-1889). Contains Yavapai-Prescott Indian Reservation, Yavapai-Apache Nation (Camp Verde), and Fort Whipple VA Medical Center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yavapai County is one of Arizona's four original counties and the parent of three others — Apache, Coconino, and Maricopa. Prescott was Arizona's first territorial capital (1864) and has the richest territorial-era records in the state. County Recorder in Prescott has deeds from 1864. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. Sharlot Hall Museum in Prescott is an outstanding research facility with territorial governor records, manuscripts, photographs, and maps. The Prescott National Forest and Tonto National Forest cover large areas — BLM/GLO records for homesteading. Jerome copper mining district (United Verde Mine) generated extensive mining and labor records. Camp Verde area has military post records (1865-1891) at NARA. Prescott's Whiskey Row and community were well-documented in the Prescott newspapers (Arizona Miner, from 1864). The Yavapai-Prescott and Yavapai-Apache tribes maintain separate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index and document images available from 1864 through county recorder portal

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1864 — the oldest continuous county deed records in Arizona. Online index and images available. Includes mining claims from numerous districts.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact; Arizona's oldest county records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1864 — the oldest county marriage records in Arizona.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1864. Includes probate for areas later carved into Apache, Coconino, and Maricopa counties.

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR / Sharlot Hall Museum / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Arizona's first newspaper (1864). Territorial politics, mining news, Indian campaigns, and community events. Published by territorial government initially.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially digitized; originals at Sharlot Hall Museum

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Sharlot Hall Museum, Prescott
source_profiles.coverage_notes: First territorial court district. Naturalization records, criminal cases (many related to mining disputes and frontier violence), and civil matters. Some governors' papers also at Sharlot Hall.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Split between ASLAPR and Sharlot Hall Museum

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Yavapai Cemetery Association
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Citizens Cemetery (Prescott, from 1864), Jerome Cemetery, and numerous mining camp and ranch cemeteries. Fort Whipple National Cemetery.
Post returns, personnel records, and Indian campaign records from Fort Whipple (established 1863 with territorial government). Now VA Medical Center.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Holdings at NARA; some at Sharlot Hall

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of four original Arizona Territory counties (1864). La Paz County carved from Yuma in 1983. Strategic Colorado River crossing — Yuma Territorial Prison (1876-1909) was a major landmark. Contains Fort Yuma Indian Reservation (Quechan) and Cocopah Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yuma County is one of Arizona's four original counties, anchored by the historic Colorado River crossing at Yuma. The Yuma Territorial Prison (1876-1909) inmate records are at ASLAPR and online — a unique source for criminal justice research. County Recorder in Yuma has deeds from 1864. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. The Arizona Historical Society Yuma branch holds Sanguinetti House Museum collections and regional manuscripts. Fort Yuma military records (1850-1883) at NARA predate Arizona Territory. The Quechan (Fort Yuma) and Cocopah tribes maintain separate records. Yuma was a critical river crossing for Gold Rush emigration (1849-1850s) — overland trail diaries reference this area. Southern Pacific Railroad records document Yuma as a division point. Agricultural irrigation records from the Yuma Reclamation Project (1904) document farming settlers. La Paz County (created 1983) records prior to that date are in Yuma County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and images available through county recorder office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1864 to present. Includes pre-1983 records for La Paz County area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact; among Arizona's oldest county records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1864. Includes pre-1983 La Paz County area marriages.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1864.

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Yuma Territorial Prison State Historic Park
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Inmate records including photographs, physical descriptions, crime, sentence, and biographical information. Over 3,000 inmates served time. Partially digitized.
Yuma's primary newspaper. Territorial-era legal notices, river navigation reports, military movements, and community events.
Post returns, personnel records from Fort Yuma. Established 1850 — predates Arizona Territory. Records of Colorado River operations and Quechan relations.
1860 census (New Mexico Territory), then 1870 forward as Arizona Territory. Quechan and Cocopah reservation populations on separate schedules.
